draft.introduction: Mark 11:1–19 combines the triumphal entry with the cursing of the fig tree and the cleansing of the temple. Mark sandwiches these events to create a commentary on Israel’s fruitlessness. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: And those who went before and those who followed were shouting, “Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David! Hosanna in the highest!”
translation.original_text: και οι προαγοντες και οι ακολουθουντες εκραζον ωσαννα

translation.text: And they came to Jerusalem. And he entered the temple and began to drive out those who sold and those who bought in the temple, and he overturned the tables of the money-changers. And he was teaching and saying to them, “Is it not written: My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations? But you have made it a den of robbers.”
translation.original_text: και ερχονται εις ιεροσολυμα και εισελθων εις το ιερον

translator_notes.note: The phrase “for all the nations” (πασιν τοις εθνεσιν), from Isaiah 56:7, is present in Mark but absent from Matthew’s parallel. Mark’s inclusion emphasizes the universal scope of the temple’s intended purpose and its corruption.
